nativeRange.habitat: Sandy shoreline zones of Lake Tanganyika at depths of 2–25 m; found exclusively among accumulated empty shells of the endemic snail Neothauma tanganyicense on open sand beds

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (5+ cm deep) for digging and shell repositioning
multiple empty Neothauma tanganyicense or escargot (Helix aspersa) shells — at least 1 per fish plus several spares
open sandy areas between shell clusters to allow territory establishment
minimal dense rockwork to avoid blocking sightlines between pairs or harems
no tall plants that root in the sand bed (may be uprooted by digging)

diet.notes: Micro-predator in the wild, feeding on small crustaceans, invertebrates, and zooplankton over open sand. In the aquarium, accepts high-quality small-pellet or micro-pellet foods, frozen cyclops, frozen or live brine shrimp (adults and nauplii), daphnia, and Moina. Variety improves coloration and conditioning. Avoid coarse pellets larger than the fish's mouth gape. Feed twice daily in small portions.

goodWith: Altolamprologus calvus (fw-fish-159) — natural predator relationship creates biological population control in a species tank or Tanganyikan community
Julidochromis ornatus or J. transcriptus (occupies upper rock zone, minimal territory conflict)
Paracyprichromis nigripinnis (open-water schooler, does not enter shell territory)
Neolamprologus multifasciatus (separate shell colonies in larger tanks)
other Lamprologus shell-dwellers in sufficiently large tanks with distinct shell-colony spacing
avoidWith: large or boisterous cichlids that will uproot shells or harass pairs
Tropheus or Petrochromis spp. (territorial conflicts, different water-flow microhabitat)
general community fish (soft-water tetras, livebearers, gouramis) incompatible with hard alkaline Tanganyikan water
large predatory catfish that can access the shell bed
crayfish or large snails that compete for or destroy shells

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Lamprologus ocellatus. Standard FishBase photo, check current availability on the species summary page.
Wikimedia Commons image; confirm license and photographer attribution before use.

summary: A tiny Tanganyikan shell-dwelling cichlid (4–5 cm) that lives, spawns, and retreats within empty gastropod shells on open sand. Requires hard alkaline water (pH 8.0–9.0) and multiple escargot or Neothauma shells. Males defend a small shell-cluster territory in monogamous pairs or 1:2 harems. Easy to breed; often paired with Altolamprologus calvus for natural population control.
careNotes: The Ocellatus Shell Dweller is one of the most popular Lake Tanganyika shell-dwellers due to its tiny footprint and fascinating shell-related behavior. A species tank or dedicated Tanganyikan biotope of 60–80 L (60 cm minimum length) can house one pair or a 1:2 male-to-female trio. A 1:2 male:female ratio (harem) is also practised; single male monogamous pairs are reported in smaller tanks. Provide fine sand at least 5 cm deep — fish actively excavate pits around their shells and will move lighter shells. Supply significantly more shells than fish: the standard recommendation is at least one shell per fish plus several spares to allow females to choose and males to offer alternatives. Neothauma tanganyicense shells are the natural substrate, but common escargot (Helix aspersa) shells of appropriate size (3–4 cm diameter for females, 5–6 cm for males) are widely used and accepted. Water chemistry must remain hard (gH 10–20, kH 8–18) and alkaline (pH 8.0–9.0), similar to Lake Tanganyika itself; crushed coral or aragonite substrate or in-filter media helps buffer pH upwards. Temperature 24–27 °C. The species has two notable color forms in the hobby: the wild-type 'Gold' or standard ocellatus with yellow-gold flanks and iridescent ocelli on the caudal peduncle, and the less common 'Blue' form sometimes associated with the Lamprologus speciosus / multipunctatus complex (true L. ocellatus is a single species — blue morphs sold as 'Blue Ocellatus' may represent regional variants or mislabelled L. speciosus). Lamprologus speciosus is a closely related sister species with similar husbandry requirements. Both forms should not be mixed to avoid hybridisation. Conspecific aggression between males is intense when shell territories overlap — in tanks under 90 cm, keep only one male. The well-documented pairing with Altolamprologus calvus exploits the natural predator–prey dynamic: calvus picks off excess fry, providing biological population management without requiring the aquarist to intervene; calvus confines itself to rockwork and ignores adult shell-dwellers.
breedingNotes: One of the easiest Tanganyikan cichlids to breed in captivity. The female selects and cleans the interior of a shell, then the male fertilises the eggs inside. Clutches are typically 20–50 eggs; incubation and early fry guarding occur entirely within the shell. The female guards the shell while the male defends the broader territory perimeter. Fry emerge after 2–3 weeks and initially remain close to the shell entrance, retreating inside when threatened. Feed emerging fry with baby brine shrimp nauplii and micro-crushed pellet. Prolific — pairs can spawn every 4–6 weeks, leading to rapid population growth in absence of predators; Altolamprologus calvus tankmates are frequently recommended as natural population control. Separate colonies in species-only setups if population growth becomes problematic.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Lamprologus ocellatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (males to 5.5 cm, females to 4 cm), temperature 24–27°C, pH 8.0–9.0, hardness 179–357 ppm (≈10–20 dH), native range (sandy shorelines, Lake Tanganyika), shell-dwelling ecology, diet (small crustaceans, invertebrates), breeding notes, and compatible species including Altolamprologus calvus.

additional.name: FishBase — Lamprologus ocellatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), adult size (max 5.5 cm TL), native range (Lake Tanganyika), temperature range (24–27°C), and depth range (2–25 m on sandy shell-bed zones). Confirmed monotypic species status and distinction from L. speciosus.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Shell Dweller Cichlids
additional.notes: Confirmed tank size recommendations (20 gal / ~75 L), shell type guidance (escargot shells), sand substrate requirement, pH 8.0–9.0, hardness buffering via crushed coral, 1:2 male-to-female ratio advice, feeding on small frozen foods, and population management via calvus pairing.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Lamprologus ocellatus care
additional.notes: Supplemental source. Confirmed shell-specific behaviour, female shell-guarding and male territory defence, Neothauma tanganyicense as natural shell, escargot as practical alternative, harem keeping, and breeding cycle details (clutch size, fry emergence timeline). Noted Gold and Blue colour forms in trade.

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum — Lamprologus ocellatus species profile
additional.notes: Community expert source confirming water parameters, shell density recommendations (1 shell per fish plus spares), L. speciosus as sister species, mixed-morph hybridisation risk, and population control pairing with Altolamprologus calvus.

fish.breedingNotes: Prolific cave/shell brooder; female spawns inside a chosen shell, male guards territory perimeter. Clutches of 20–50 eggs; fry emerge in ~2–3 weeks. Baby brine shrimp nauplii recommended for first foods. Pairs breed every 4–6 weeks; calvus tankmates provide natural population control.
